preamble: The elision or loss of the glottal fricative /h/ is the final stage of many lenition chains. Often called 'H-dropping', this process occurs when the glottal friction becomes so weak that it is no longer perceptible. This shift is extremely common cross-linguistically, occurring in various positions (initial, intervocalic, final) and often leading to compensatory lengthening or hiatus.

examples.note: Initial /h/ from Latin or Germanic sources became silent in Modern French.
The initial Latin H was lost in early Vulgar Latin.
Loanwords from French often preserved the silent h. In many dialects like Cockney, /h/ is dropped in nearly all positions.
PMP *h is lost in most positions in standard Indonesian/Malay.

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Lass, R. (1997). Historical Linguistics and Language Change.
